statement: The EEA Agreement gives Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway access to the EU's single market by incorporating relevant EU legislation into their national law. The EEA covers approximately 70–75% of the EU acquis by volume (measured by number of legal acts), focusing on the 'four freedoms': free movement of goods, persons, services, and capital. However, major policy areas are excluded from the EEA: the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), the Common Fisheries Policy (CFP), the Customs Union (Iceland maintains its own customs tariff), the Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP), Justice and Home Affairs (partially — Schengen is separate), Economic and Monetary Union (euro), and the EU's common trade policy (Iceland conducts its own trade agreements with non-EU countries). The EFTA Surveillance Authority (ESA) and EFTA Court provide institutional oversight for EEA EFTA states, mirroring the European Commission and CJEU respectively. As of 2025, over 13,000 EU legal acts had been incorporated into the EEA Agreement since 1994, with approximately 200–400 new acts incorporated annually through EEA Joint Committee decisions.
source_name: EFTA Secretariat — The EEA Agreement; ESA Internal Market Scoreboard; EEA Joint Committee
caveats: The '70–75%' figure is an approximation that depends on how one measures acquis coverage (number of acts, pages, or policy areas). By number of policy areas, the EEA covers a smaller proportion because entire domains (agriculture, fisheries, monetary policy, foreign policy, defence) are excluded. By volume of individual legal acts, the coverage is higher because single market legislation is extensive. Some commentators say Icelanders 'already implement most EU rules' — this is true in the narrow sense of single market regulation but misses the substantial areas of EU policy that fall entirely outside the EEA.

related_entries.statement: As of 2025, approximately 70% of EU internal market legislation has been incorporated into Icelandic law through the EEA Agreement. This covers free movement of goods, services, capital, and persons, but excludes fisheries, agriculture, and justice/home affairs.

The EU has adopted a vast body of legislation since its founding. As of end 2024, the EUR-Lex database records approximately 146,700 regulations and 4,600 directives adopted since 1958, totalling over 151,000 binding legal acts. During the EEA period (1994–2024), the EU adopted roughly 57,100 regulations (averaging 1,842 per year) and 2,626 directives (85 per year). Legislative output has declined from a peak of approximately 4,000+ regulations per year in the late 1980s to around 1,200 per year in 2019–2023.

Iceland implements approximately 75% of EU single market legislation through the EEA Agreement without having any voting rights in the EU institutions that draft and adopt these laws. The EEA Joint Committee, where Iceland has a formal seat, operates by consensus but in practice rubber-stamps EU legislation — Iceland has never exercised its Article 102 'right of reservation' to veto an EEA-relevant act. The EFTA pillar's consultative role is limited to providing input before legislation is finalised, with no amendment power. This arrangement has been described as a 'fax democracy' by Norwegian critics — Iceland receives the laws by fax (metaphorically) and implements them without meaningful influence over their content.

claims.claim_text: Sjavarutvegur er undanskilinn EES-samningnum og vegna theirrar undanthagu geta islensk log bannath erlendum adilum ad eiga meira en 25% (eda 33% obeint) i islenskum utgerthum.
ESB hvílir á fjórum frelsisréttindum (fjórfrelsið) — þar á meðal staðfesturétti og frjálsu flæði fjármagns.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Margir samningakaflar um efnahags- og markaðsmál voru auðveldir vegna þess að Ísland er þegar í EES-samningnum.
